ocr.text: This report examines the clinical data related to a cohort of patients exhibiting symptoms consistent with potential tick-borne
illnesses. The table presents critical metrics, including "Days Sick" and "Pathogen(s)" identified, which provide insight into the
duration and nature of the illnesses reported. Notably, the data highlights cases where patients experienced a tight neck, a

symptom often associated with neurological complications of certain pathogens. 

In the observed cases, patients were
documented with varying durations of illness, with some reporting being symptomatic for as few as two days.

This rapid onset may suggest an acute phase of infection, warranting prompt medical attention. Additionally, the "Time
Attached (hrs)" metric sheds light on the potential exposure duration to ticks, which is vital for understanding the risk factors

associated with transmission. 

The relationship between the time attached and the subsequent incubation period is critical for
assessing the timeline of symptom development. Furthermore, the document notes instances of medical interventions, including
whether patients sought medical attention or underwent blood draws, which can inform future clinical practices and guidelines
for managing similar cases. Overall, this data serves as a foundation for further analysis of tick-borne disease epidemiology and
patient outcomes.
